elements.sent-2411-001: We consider the problem of predicting perturbation effects via causal models.
elements.sent-2411-002: In many applications, it is a priori unknown which mechanisms of a system are modified by an external perturbation, even though the features of the perturbation are available.
elements.sent-2411-003: For example, in genomics, some properties of a drug may be known, but not their causal effects on the regulatory pathways of cells.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose a {\em generative intervention model (GIM) that learns to map these perturbation features to distributions over atomic interventions in a jointly-estimated causal model.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Contrary to prior approaches, this enables us to predict the distribution shifts of {\em unseen perturbation features while gaining insights about their {\em mechanistic effects in the underlying data-generating process.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: On synthetic data and scRNA-seq drug perturbation data, GIMs achieve robust out-of-distribution predictions on par with unstructured approaches, while effectively inferring the underlying perturbation mechanisms, often better than other causal inference methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: Decision-making and experiment design in the sciences requires predicting how systems respond to perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-008: Since testing all perturbations in a design space is often intractable, expensive, or unethical, scientists need data-driven approaches for predicting the effects of unseen perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, predicting how cells react to novel molecules can facilitate drug design in the vast space of candidate molecules \citep{sadybekov2023computational,tejada2023causal .}
elements.sent-2411-010: As in all learning tasks, however, generalization to unseen instances requires an inductive bias \citep{IbrHas81 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Perturbation effects are commonly predicted with either unstructured {\em black-box models that predict the distribution shifts resulting from perturbations end-to-end or structured {\em mechanistic models that characterize shifts as changes to an underlying causal data-generating process.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, when predicting drug effects, they use features like dosage levels or molecular footprints as covariates to directly predict the perturbation response.
elements.sent-2411-013: This is practical because it allows making predictions for new perturbation features.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, these approaches do not explicitly model the underlying data-generating mechanisms and how they change under a perturbation \citep{scholkopf2022causality .}
elements.sent-2411-015: As a consequence, unstructured models are challenging to interpret, difficult to use in experiment design, and impractical to use for perturbations with different feature spaces.
elements.sent-2411-016: By contrast, mechanistic models characterize distribution shifts as interventions on a structured or causal model of the system (\eg, \citealp{pearl2009causality,parascandolo2018learning,gonzalez2024combinatorial,roohani2024predicting ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: This perspective provides explanations for the predicted shifts as it explicitly infers the data-generating mechanisms and how they change under an intervention.
elements.sent-2411-018: Crucially, a causal model generalizes to new perturbations on the system---assuming we know the {\em atomic interventions on the system components induced by a given perturbation.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This assumption underlies most mechanistic approaches today and constrains them to settings where the perturbation targets are known \citep[e.g.,][]{maathuis2010predicting or, if unknown, inferred without considering the perturbation features \citep[e.g.,][]{mooij2020joint .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: By not leveraging the features of a perturbation as covariates, causal models currently cannot make predictions for novel, non-atomic perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this work, we propose a causal modeling approach for predicting the effects of general perturbations, whose features may not directly reveal how the perturbation modifies the causal generative process.
elements.sent-2411-022: To achieve this, we train a generative intervention model (\ours) that maps the perturbation features to a distribution over atomic interventions in a jointly-learned causal model.
elements.sent-2411-023: The intervened causal generative process then models the distribution after perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-024: Since the \ours is shared across perturbations, it can systematically generalize to unseen perturbation features, similar to unstructured approaches, while providing mechanistic insights into how the data-generating process is altered.
elements.sent-2411-025: To evaluate the learned internal structure and perturbation predictions separately, our experiments evaluate \ourss in causal discovery, intervention target identification, and end-to-end perturbation effect prediction on synthetic data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Especially in nonlinear systems, \ourss outperform most causal inference approaches in structure and target discovery, while predicting distribution shifts on par with unstructured approaches in out-of-distribution settings.
elements.sent-2411-027: On single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data of drug perturbations \citep{srivatsan2020massively , we show that \ourss can generalize effectively to new drug dosages and characterize the distribution shifts on a selection of genes quantitatively and qualitatively more accurately than the baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We study a system of variables $\boldx \in \RR^d$ with density $p(\boldx)$ under perturbations of its generative process.
elements.sent-2411-029: A perturbation on $\boldx$ is represented by a feature vector $\perturb \in \RR^p$ that fully characterizes how $p(\boldx)$ shifts under the perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-030: For example, $\perturb$ may capture the chemical properties of a drug perturbing a cell or the context of an experiment.
elements.sent-2411-031: For a given perturbation $\perturb$, we observe a data matrix $\rmX$ of \iid samples from $p(\boldx; \perturb)$, the perturbed density under $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-032: Given an unseen perturbation $\perturb^*$ and a collection $\Dcal$ of $K$ observed environments $\Dcal = \{(\rmX^{(k) , \perturb^{(k) )\}_{k=1 ^{K}$, we aim to model \begin{equation}p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb^*) \, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Our proposed framework leverages a causal model to characterize the (posterior predictive) perturbed density $p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb)$ for arbitrary $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Before introducing our approach, we first summarize relevant work on perturbation modeling and causal inference and discuss how they relate to our work.
elements.sent-2411-035: Autoencoders typically represent distribution shifts induced by perturbations in a learned latent space, some only applying to combinations of known perturbations \citep{lopez2018deep, lotfollahi2019scgen , others generalizing to new perturbations by conditioning on $\perturb$ \citep{yu2022perturbnet, hetzel2022predicting, lotfollahi2023predicting .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Optimal transport methods like CondOT \citep{bunne2022supervised, bunne2023learning learn transport maps and generalize by conditioning the map on $\perturb$.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Domain-specific foundation models \citep{cui2024scgpt, hao2024large learn similar conditioning tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-038: These approaches provide little insights into the system and perturbation mechanisms and are often highly specific to biochemistry.
elements.sent-2411-039: Most methods require knowledge of how the perturbations alter the causal mechanisms and focus on inferring $\model$, e.g., through conditional independence tests \citep{spirtes2000causation or model assumptions \citep{chickering2002optimal, zheng2018dags, yang2018characterizing .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Recent approaches additionally aim at inferring the atomic modifications in $\model$ that underlie the observations $\Dcal$ \citep{mooij2020joint, brouillard2020differentiable, hagele2023bacadi, squires20permutation .}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, these methods do not infer any perturbation mechanisms beyond the training data.
elements.sent-2411-042: As a result, they are unable to predict the effects of {\em unseen perturbations $\perturb^*$, leaving $p(\boldx ; \perturb^*)$ undefined for general perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-043: To do so, existing methods would require knowing the specific atomic interventions in $\model$ caused by $\perturb^*$.
elements.sent-2411-044: However, these either do not model perturbations \citep{parascandolo2018learning,pervez2024mechanistic or are highly domain-specific, like CellOracle \citep{kamimoto2023dissecting , GEARS \citep{roohani2024predicting or PDGrapher \citep{gonzalez2024combinatorial , who rely on expert knowledge to construct a graph neural network, which is not applicable in general.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In this section, we introduce generative intervention models, which induce a novel class of densities $p(\boldx; \perturb)$ for perturbation modeling.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our approach leverages the inductive bias of a causal model, whose data-generating process characterizes the density of $\boldx$ under any perturbation $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For this, we first describe causal models and how perturbations can be modeled as local modifications of the causal generative process.
elements.sent-2411-048: We then show how introducing an explicit generative model of these local modifications allows linking the perturbation features $\perturb$ to the causal model, enabling us to characterize $p(\boldx; \perturb^*)$ for arbitrary perturbations $\perturb^*$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, we assume that $\boldx$ is generated by a structural causal model (SCM; \citealp{pearl2009causality ) with directed acyclic causal graph $\graph \in \{0, 1\}^{d \times d}$ and parameters $\gtheta$ that encode the causal relationships among the variables $\boldx$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The induced distribution $p(\boldx; \graph, \gtheta)$ factorizes as a product of independent causal mechanisms \begin{equation}\label{eq:cbn-factorization} x_i \sim p_i(\gxi \given \gxpai ; \gtheta) \, ,\end{equation} \looseness-1 with $p(\boldx; \graph, \gtheta) = \prod_{i=1 ^{d p(x_i \given \gxpai ; \gtheta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Each conditional distribution models a variable $\gxi$ given its direct causes (parents) $\gxpai$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Under this generative process, each $\gxi$ is independent of its nondescendants in $\graph$ given its parents $\gxpai$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Perturbations can be modeled as sparse mechanism shifts in the causal data-generating process since perturbations typically only modify few {\em causal mechanisms of a system at once \citep{peters2017elements, scholkopf2022causality .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: To capture this, we model perturbations of $\boldx$ as local ({\em atomic ) {\em interventions on a sparse subset of the conditionals in \Cref{eq:cbn-factorization .}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: An intervention targeting a variable $\gxi$ replaces its causal mechanism with an interventional mechanism \begin{equation}\label{eq:cbn-intervention} x_i \sim \tilde{p}_i(\gxi \given \gxpai ; \gtheta, \gthetaintv)\end{equation} with additional parameters $\gthetaintv$, while not modifying any other conditionals of the factorization over $\graph$.
elements.sent-2411-056: We may model various types of interventions, such as {\em hard and {\em soft interventions, which differ in how they modify the observational conditionals (\Cref{app:additional_background ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We denote the variable indices targeted by an intervention by $\gintv \in \{0, 1\}^d$ and write \begin{equation}\model = \{\graph, \gtheta\}\, , \quad\quad \quad\quad \intv = \{\gintv, \gthetaintv \} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: For example, $\perturb$ could represent the molecular features of a drug and $\intv$ its targets and effects in the generative process of $\boldx$ (e.g., gene expressions).
elements.sent-2411-059: Thus, $p(\boldx ; \model)$ denotes the causal generative model and $p(\boldx ; \model, \intv)$ the model perturbed by the atomic intervention $\intv$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Next, we discuss how to bridge this modeling perspective to general perturbations $\perturb$ and ultimately model $p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$ for arbitrary features $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, to model the general perturbations $p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$, the perturbation features $\perturb$ have to be linked to atomic interventions $\intv$ such that, intuitively, $p(\boldx ; \model, \intv) = p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$ for some $\intv$ depending on $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Existing causal methods \citep[e.g.,][]{mooij2020joint,hagele2023bacadi infer $\intv$ given samples from $p(\boldx; \perturb)$, but this does not allow making predictions for new features $\perturb$ in practical prediction tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-063: This generative intervention model (\ours) is fully parameterized by $\gimparam$ and shared across perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-064: It characterizes the distributions of the intervention targets and parameters conditioned on $\perturb$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:gim-amortized}  p(\intv ; \perturb, \gimparam) = p\big(\gintv ; \gim(\perturb)\big) p\big(\gthetaintv ; \gimm(\gintv, \perturb)\big) \, , \end{equation} \looseness0 where $\gim$ and $\gimm$ are arbitrary functions.
elements.sent-2411-065: Contrary to \citet{hagele2023bacadi , we do not seek to infer $\intv$ from $\Dcal$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Instead, we view the interventions $\intv$ as latent variables that ought to be marginalized out when modeling $p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb)$, contributing to the overall epistemic uncertainty given $\Dcal$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Specifically, \ourss model the generative process of $\boldx$ under a perturbation $\perturb$ by marginalizing over its distribution of $\intv$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:gim} \hspace*{-5pt} p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb, \gimparam) = \int p(\boldx \given \intv; \model) p(\intv ; \perturb, \gimparam) \odif \intv ~~ = \iint p(\boldx \given \gintv, \gthetaintv ; \model) p\big(\gintv ; \gim(\perturb)\big) p\big(\gthetaintv ; \gimm(\gintv, \perturb)\big) \odif \gintv \odif \gthetaintv \, . \notag\end{equation} Unlike previous causal inference approaches \citep{mooij2020joint,hagele2023bacadi , \ourss can predict $p(\boldx; \perturb)$ for arbitrary unseen features $\perturb$.}

elements.sent-2411-189: Atomic interventions in a causal model refer to any external manipulations or changes that modify the variables or causal mechanisms within the network \citep{peters2017elements, vowels2022d .}
elements.sent-2411-190: Formally, an intervention on a variable $x_i$ corresponds to replacing the local conditional distribution $p_i$ in \Cref{eq:cbn-factorization by a new interventional distribution $\tilde{p _i$, while the conditionals of the variables not subject to the intervention remain unchanged \citep{scholkopf2021toward, scholkopf2022causality .}}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Thus, interventions are sparse or local in nature, meaning that they only affect the targeted variables and do not directly influence the other variables of a system.
elements.sent-2411-192: We typically distinguish between two types of atomic interventions: soft and hard interventions \citep{peters2017elements, pearl2009causality, Eberhardt2005OnTN .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Soft (or imperfect) interventions modify the conditional probability distributions of the intervened-upon variable without changing the structure of the causal graph $\graph$.
elements.sent-2411-194: This means that the functional dependencies on the parents remain.
elements.sent-2411-195: One example for soft interventions are so-called shift interventions \citep{rothenhausler2015backshift , which modify the conditional distributions of the target variables according to a shift vector $\gthetaintv = (\gthetaintv_1, ...,\gthetaintv_d) \in \mathbb{R ^{d}$, so that \begin{equation}\label{eq:shift_interv} \tilde{p}_i(x_i = x + \gthetaintv_{i} \given \gxpai ; \gtheta, \gthetaintv) = p_i(x_i = x \given \gxpai; \gtheta).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Hard (or perfect) interventions, on the other hand, eliminate the dependencies of the intervened variables on their causal parents so that the interventional conditional simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{eq:hard_interv} \tilde{p}_i(x_i | \gxpai; \gtheta, \gthetaintv) = \tilde{p}_i(x_i |\gthetaintv),\end{equation} ultimately altering the structure of the causal graph.
elements.sent-2411-197: Real world examples from biology include perfect gene knockout experiments for hard interventions and gene silencing for soft intervention \citep{squires20permutation .}

elements.sent-2411-204: In the following section, we provide details on our synthetic data generation, preprocessing the Sciplex 3 dataset and implementation details on \ourss and all baseline approaches.
elements.sent-2411-205: For our synthetic data generation, we use linear and nonlinear Gaussian causal mechanisms as defined in \Cref{eq:lin_gaussian_cbn and \Cref{eq:nonlin_gaussian_cbn , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: For linear causal mechanisms, linear functions model a variable's dependency on its parents and thus the parameters $\gtheta$ represent these regression coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-207: For data generation, we randomly sample the parameters $\gtheta$ uniformly from $[-3, -0.25] \cup [0.25, 3]$ to make sure they are bounded away from zero and exclude non-significant relationships.
elements.sent-2411-208: Further, we always use a constant variance for all variables $x_i$, thus $\exp(\sigma_i)^2= \exp(\sigma)^2=0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-209: Similarly, for nonlinear causal mechanisms, a variable's dependency on its causal parents is defined via a nonlinear MLP.
elements.sent-2411-210: We thus have one MLP for each variable, so we have in total $d$ networks.
elements.sent-2411-211: We use MLPs with one hidden layer with $5$ units and the $\tanh$ activation function.
elements.sent-2411-212: We sample the weights $\gtheta$ from a standard Gaussian with mean 0 and variance 1.
elements.sent-2411-214: We generate random atomic interventions, by first sampling the intervention targets $\boldI$.
elements.sent-2411-215: Specifically, we uniformly sample the targets from all possible subsets of variabels containing exactly one or two elements.
elements.sent-2411-216: Next, we use randomly sampled Hill functions to compute the intervention parameters $\gthetaintv$ acting on a fixed target variable.
elements.sent-2411-217: For each randomly-sampled target variable $x_i$, we separately sample $\lambda_i \sim \mathcal{U [-6, -2] \cup [2,6]$ and define its Hill function as $h_{\lambda_i (c) = \lambda_i / (1 + ( 0.75/{c )^{4 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: Given the targets and their Hill functions, we then generate four distinct interventional environments for training by evaluating $h_{\lambda_i (c)$ at the dosages $c \in \{0.5, 1, 1.5, 2\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Akin to drug perturbations, each of these four environments shares the same targets $\gintv$ but has different intervention effects $\gthetaintv$, which are governed by the same Hill function evaluated at the different dosages $c$.
elements.sent-2411-220: We consider two types of atomic interventions: hard and shift interventions, which are detailed in \Cref{app:additional_background .}
elements.sent-2411-221: For settings with hard interventions, the intervention parameters $\gthetaintv$, which are specified via the Hill functions, describe the mean of the interventional conditional distribution for a targeted variable.
elements.sent-2411-222: Specifically, the interventional conditional of a targeted variable is defined as $\tilde{p _i(x_i | \gthetaintv) = \Ncal(x_i; \gthetaintv, \exp(\sigma_{interv ))$.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Contrary to the observational conditionals, we choose $\exp(\sigma_{interv )^2 = 0.5$ to ensure that the interventional distributions remain distinct from the observational ones even at small dosages, where the mean of the interventional conditional is close to zero, regardless of whether a variable has parents in the original graph.}
elements.sent-2411-224: For settings with shift interventions, the intervention parameters $\gthetaintv$, which are specified via the Hill functions, merely shift the targeted variables without introducing additional noise as defined in \Cref{eq:shift_interv \citep{rothenhausler2015backshift .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: The training dataset consists of a total of $160$ perturbational datasets (corresponding to $40$ Hill functions considered at $4$ different dosages), where each one has $n_k=50$ samples, and one observational dataset with $n_0 = 800$ samples.
elements.sent-2411-226: The partially out-of-distribution test dataset consists of $200$ perturbational contexts corresponding to the same $40$ hill functions and intervention targets from the training dataset, but evaluated at $5$ different dosages, $c \in \{0.25, 0.75, 1.25, 1.75, 2.25\}$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Finally, the fully out-of-distribution test dataset consists of 80 perturbational contexts corresponding to $20$ newly sampled hill functions, which are evaluated at dosages $c \in \{0.5, 1, 1.5, 2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-228: Single-cell RNA sequencing data, like the SciPlex3 dataset \citep{srivatsan2020massively , usually comes with technical particularities that require careful preprocessing.}
elements.sent-2411-229: Thus, we apply standard preprocessing methods.
elements.sent-2411-230: First, we normalize the data such that each cell has the same total count, equal to the median count of the unperturbed data.
elements.sent-2411-231: We then filter cells to retain only those with at least 200 counts, and genes to keep only those expressed in at least 20 cells.
elements.sent-2411-232: Next, we apply a logarithmic transformation, $\tilde{x = \log(1 + x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Since we focus on a subset of genes, we first identify the top 1000 highly variable genes, and subsequently reduce this set to the top 50 marker genes.
elements.sent-2411-234: To determine these, we rank all genes and select those with the highest absolute scores.
elements.sent-2411-235: We limit our analysis to four drugs (Belinostat, Dacinostat, Givinostat, and Quinostat) from the epigenetic regulation pathway.
elements.sent-2411-236: For each drug perturbation, we construct corresponding perturbation features by concatenating the one-hot encoding of the drugs, one-hot encoding of the dosages, and rescaled dosage values, ensuring these values fall within $[0, 1]$.
elements.sent-2411-237: Specifically, we apply a hard-coded mapping of the dosages given by: $10 nM \rightarrow 0.2$, $100nM \rightarrow 0.4$, $1 \mu M \rightarrow 0.6$, and $10 \mu \rightarrow 0.8M$.
elements.sent-2411-238: Thus, the perturbation vector is a 9-dimensional vector, with $\perturb \in \{0,1\}^{4 \times \{0,1\}^{4 \times [0, 1]$.}}

elements.sent-2411-350: Decision-making and experiment design in the sciences require predicting how systems respond to perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-351: Especially in nonlinear systems, \ourss outperform most causal inference approaches in structure and intervention target discovery, while predicting distribution shifts on par with unstructured approaches in out-of-distribution settings.
elements.sent-2411-352: A perturbation of $\boldx$ is represented by a feature vector $\perturb \in \RR^p$, which fully characterizes how $p(\boldx)$ shifts under the perturbation; we denote the resulting perturbed density as $p(\boldx; \perturb)$.
elements.sent-2411-353: The perturbation features $\perturb$ are observable and can, for instance, represent the chemical properties of a drug applied to cells or the context of an experiment.
elements.sent-2411-354: We observe data from $K$ experimental environments, each defined by a distinct perturbation applied to the system.
elements.sent-2411-355: For each environment $k$, we observe the corresponding perturbation features $\perturb^{(k)}$ and the system's response, given by a data matrix $\rmX^{(k)}$ of \iid samples from $p(\boldx; \perturb)$, e.g. the gene expression.
elements.sent-2411-356: Together, these observations form the full dataset $\Dcal = \{(\rmX^{(k) , \perturb^{(k) )\}_{k=1 ^{K}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-357: Given our dataset and an unseen perturbation $\perturb^*$, we aim to model \begin{equation}p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb^*) \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-358: Before introducing our approach, we summarize relevant work on perturbation modeling and causal inference and discuss how they relate to our work.
elements.sent-2411-359: These approaches provide little insight into the system and perturbation mechanisms and are often highly specific to biochemistry.
elements.sent-2411-360: Most methods assume that, for each perturbation, it is known how the system’s causal mechanisms are modified.
elements.sent-2411-361: Under this assumption, $\model$ is estimated, e.g., through conditional independence tests \citep{spirtes2000causation or model assumptions \citep{chickering2002optimal, zheng2018dags, yang2018characterizing, lorch2021dibs .}}
elements.sent-2411-362: However, these methods cannot be applied when the underlying atomic modifications are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-363: Recent approaches aim at additionally inferring the atomic modifications that underlie the observations $\Dcal$ \citep{mooij2020joint, brouillard2020differentiable, hagele2023bacadi, squires20permutation .}
elements.sent-2411-364: However, these methods infer the changes only for the perturbations in $\Dcal$ and treat each perturbation independently, without leveraging the associated perturbation features $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-365: However, these works either do not model perturbations \citep{parascandolo2018learning,pervez2024mechanistic or are highly domain-specific, like CellOracle \citep{kamimoto2023dissecting , GEARS \citep{roohani2024predicting or PDGrapher \citep{gonzalez2024combinatorial , who rely on expert knowledge to construct a graph neural network, which is not applicable in general settings.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: In this section, we introduce generative intervention models, which form a novel class of densities $p(\boldx; \perturb)$ for perturbation modeling.
elements.sent-2411-367: To begin, we first describe causal models and how perturbations can be modeled as local modifications of the causal generative process.
elements.sent-2411-368: Then, we show how introducing an explicit generative model of these local modifications allows us to link the perturbation features $\perturb$ to the causal model.
elements.sent-2411-369: This enables us to sample from $p(\boldx; \perturb^*)$ for arbitrary perturbations $\perturb^*$.
elements.sent-2411-370: Perturbations can be modeled as sparse mechanism shifts of the causal data-generating process, since perturbations typically only modify a few {\em causal mechanisms of a system at once \citep{peters2017elements, scholkopf2022causality .}}
elements.sent-2411-371: An intervention that targets a variable $\gxi$ replaces its causal mechanism with an interventional mechanism \begin{equation}\label{eq:cbn-intervention} x_i \sim \tilde{p}_i(\gxi \given \gxpai ; \gtheta, \gthetaintv)\end{equation} with additional parameters $\gthetaintv$, while not modifying any other conditionals of the factorization over $\graph$.
elements.sent-2411-372: We may model various types of interventions, such as {\em hard or {\em soft interventions, which differ in how they modify the observational conditionals (\Cref{app:additional_background ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-373: For example, $\perturb$ could represent the molecular features of a drug and $\intv$ its targets and effects in the generative process of the gene expressions $\boldx$ of cells, which is characterized by $\model$.
elements.sent-2411-374: In summary, $p(\boldx ; \model)$ denotes the causal generative model and $p(\boldx ; \model, \intv)$ the model perturbed by the atomic intervention $\intv$.
elements.sent-2411-375: Next, we discuss how to bridge this approach to general perturbations $\perturb$ and ultimately model $p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$ for arbitrary features $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-376: However, these atomic interventions are not observed in our problem setting, as we only observe the perturbation features $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-377: Thus, to model the general perturbations $p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$, the perturbation features $\perturb$ have to be linked to an atomic intervention $\intv$ such that, intuitively, $p(\boldx ; \model, \intv) = p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb)$ for some $\intv$ depending on $\perturb$.
elements.sent-2411-378: Existing causal methods \citep[e.g.,][]{mooij2020joint,hagele2023bacadi directly infer $\intv$ given samples from $p(\boldx; \perturb)$.}
elements.sent-2411-379: In particular, they independently infer a separate $\intv$ for each perturbation in $\Dcal$, without explicitly modeling how the features $\perturb$ relate to the intervention $\intv$.
elements.sent-2411-380: As a result, they cannot infer the intervention $\intv$ for new features $\perturb$ and thus cannot make predictions for the corresponding system response in practical prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-381: Contrary to \citet{hagele2023bacadi , we do not seek to directly infer $\intv$ from $\Dcal$.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Instead, we view the interventions $\intv$ as latent variables that contribute to the overall epistemic uncertainty given $\Dcal$ and are marginalized out when modeling $p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb)$.
elements.sent-2411-383: Specifically, \ourss model the generative process of $\boldx$ under a perturbation $\perturb$ by marginalizing over its distribution of $\intv$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:gim} \hspace*{-5pt} p(\boldx ; \model, \perturb, \gimparam) = \int p(\boldx \given \intv; \model) p(\intv ; \perturb, \gimparam) \odif \intv ~~ = \iint p(\boldx \given \gintv, \gthetaintv ; \model) p\big(\gintv ; \gim(\perturb)\big) p\big(\gthetaintv ; \gimm(\gintv, \perturb)\big) \odif \gintv \odif \gthetaintv \, . \notag\end{equation} Unlike previous causal inference approaches \citep{mooij2020joint,hagele2023bacadi , \ourss can thus generalize to sampling from $p(\boldx; \perturb)$ for arbitrary unseen features $\perturb$.}
elements.sent-2411-384: From our generative model in \Cref{eq:gim , we see that this requires posterior inference of the causal model $\model$ and \ours parameters $\gimparam$, which map the perturbation features to the interventions in the causal model.}
elements.sent-2411-385: For simplicity, we consider a crude approximation of the posterior, as indicated above, and simply learn maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimates of the causal model $\model$ and the \ours parameters $\gimparam$: \begin{equation}p(\boldx \given \Dcal; \perturb) \notag = \int p(\boldx \given \model, \gimparam; \perturb) p(\model, \gimparam \given \Dcal) \odif \model \odif \gimparam \approx~ p(\boldx \given \model^*, \gimparam^*; \perturb) \label{eq:gim-predictive-distribution}\end{equation} with $\smash{\model^*, \gimparam^* = \arg \max_{\model, \gimparam \, p(\model, \gimparam \given \Dcal)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-386: We note that, since $ p(\intv \given \Dcal, \perturb) = \int p(\intv ; \perturb, \gimparam) p(\gimparam \given \Dcal) \odif \gimparam $, posterior inference for \ourss enables performing {\em amortized posterior inference of $\intv$ given $\Dcal$ downstream \citep[cf.][]{hagele2023bacadi .}}
